steps.title: Identify the sentence boundary
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: “They were not the first in England to adopt the literary modes of classical antiquity” (complete idea) and “some of the most prominent figures of the earlier Renaissance period were also influenced by ancient Greek and Roman literature” (complete idea). These must be joined with punctuation strong enough to separate two independent clauses.

steps.title: Apply the conjunctive adverb rule
steps.content: The word “however” is a conjunctive adverb that signals contrast between the clauses. Two independent clauses joined by a conjunctive adverb follow this SAT pattern: a semicolon before the adverb, then a comma after it (IC; however, IC). Choice D places the semicolon after “antiquity” and the comma after “however,” creating the correct structure: “classical antiquity ; however, some of the most prominent figures…” The other options fail: A leaves no punctuation between clauses (comma splice); B places only commas around “however” (insufficient for joining independent clauses); C places a comma before “however” and a semicolon after, which reverses the proper order and mishandles the junction. That matches choice D — D is correct.
